next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Tomas counted his steps as his first coach had taught him: *uno, dos, tres*. The rhythm had locked in before engineers ever touched his body, back when counting was the only technology available to a thirteen-year-old on a dirt track outside Quito. Three years of neural integration had not erased it. The enhancement handled stride frequency to the hundredth of a second now. It smoothed his approach pattern into a shape that felt less like running and more like discovering what running was for. But the counting stayed. His one piece of untouched equipment.

The indoor track smelled like hot rubber and recycled air. Mid-morning. A group session. Four athletes spread across the facility's straight and curve. Kai was doing light sprint work on the far lane, his stride still favoring the shoulder that had healed wrong after the blackout crash five weeks ago. Two other athletes Tomas knew only by event, a pole vaulter from the Dutch program and a middle-distance runner whose name he kept forgetting and felt bad about, occupied the curve. The high ceilings swallowed the sound of their footfalls and returned it as faint percussion, a dozen rubber impacts overlapping into a rhythm that almost held.

Tomas had called his mother last night. She'd wanted to know about the weather, so he'd described the snowmelt off the peaks, how the water ran in channels beside the road down to Meiringen, how the Alps looked different now that the ice was pulling back and exposing gray rock. His sister Valeria had grabbed the phone to announce she'd made the regional soccer team, and he'...
open_hooks_to_carry: Lena conscious during the overnight firmware rewrite: heat along the neural link, pressure behind her eyes, vision splitting into dual perspectives
Post-patch diagnostics: desync stabilizes but reaction times slower and sensory acuity blunted
The silence where the system's ambient processing used to be—Lena has never heard this silence before
Swimming laps that feel ordinary for the first time in her life
prev_tail_excerpt:
"If you start tonight, how long before the patch is in?"

He looked at her. "Installation's a few hours. Calibration goes longer. Why?"

Lena looked down at her hands. One thumb had stopped moving. She rubbed it with the other until it started again.

"Because if I go back to my room and sleep on it, I'll lose more time and call that thinking." She lifted her eyes to him. "If I'm doing it, I want it tonight."

Yusuf didn't speak for a second.

"Are you sure?"

"No," Lena said. "Set it up anyway."

He held her gaze, then nodded once.

"All right. Forty minutes."

He left with the laptop under his arm.

Kai looked at Lena. "You OK?"

She kept staring at her hands.

"No," she said. "Come with me anyway."

She stood. Kai stood with her.

In the corridor, Yusuf's steps were already receding toward the calibration bay. Lena followed the sound of his steps.
secondary_character_beats: Dara watches Lena after the patch with visible confusion—she does not understand why Lena would choose diminished performance and silence over integrated optimization
state_deltas_required: The patch installation requires consciousness through a night-long sensory ordeal; the result is uncertain
The cure works but reveals what it costs—the silence where the system was is its own form of loss
